placeholder.desktop: Search for an app you know, like: Instagram, Google Drive...

desktop: Discover a new way to use the internet, where you have more control over what you share, choose who you connect with, and participate in networks that work without relying on a central authority.
There are alternatives to common platforms, built by independent communities. They prioritize your freedom and let you use the internet more safely with less data collection and behavioral tracking.

Use tools built to guarantee your autonomy online. With them, you choose how to communicate, what to share, and who to interact with — all without giving up your privacy or depending on a single entity.
Each app has a different purpose: some focus on social networks, others on videos, text or messaging. Discover which one fits your way of using the internet and explore new possibilities.
Browse a selection of tools built with digital freedom in mind. See which ones allow more control, and find the one that best fits what you're looking for.
Decentralized platforms connect in different ways, but share the idea of a more open and collaborative internet. Here you understand how they work and why it matters.
mobile: Open source alternatives to popular apps.
Discover decentralized apps maintained by the community.
Find alternative apps to ones you already know.
Use tools built to promote your autonomy online.
Browse a selection of tools focused on digital freedom.
Discover a new way to use the internet

appModal.selfHostingTooltip: How difficult it is to self-host this app (1=Easy, 3=Advanced)

peertube.description: A video platform similar to YouTube, but decentralized with peer-to-peer streaming between instances.
peertube.longDescription: PeerTube is a decentralized video platform. Instead of a single site like YouTube, there are multiple independent instances that host videos and connect with each other. You can watch, comment, and even subscribe to channels from other instances. PeerTube uses P2P technology to help with video loading, especially during high-traffic moments. It's ideal for those who want to share videos with more freedom and less tracking.

features.1: Each instance customizes and moderates content as it sees fit
features.2: You can follow channels from other instances
features.3: No ads, trackers, or recommendation algorithms

peertube.reasonToUse: Choose PeerTube if you want to publish videos without surrendering your audience to advertising algorithms or depending on monetization rules imposed by third parties.

challenges.1: Hosting videos requires significant storage and bandwidth; a modest server may struggle with traffic spikes even with P2P assistance.
challenges.2: Content moderation is the instance administrator's responsibility, requiring time and judgment to prevent abuse.
challenges.3: Content discovery across instances is still limited — new users have difficulty finding creators outside their home instance.

mastodon.description: A social network similar to Twitter, but without ads, no algorithm, and more control for users.
mastodon.longDescription: Mastodon is a microblogging social network where you can post text, images, videos and polls. It operates through multiple independent instances that connect with each other. You choose which instance to join, or you can create your own. The feed is always chronological, with no algorithms or ads, and each community has its own rules. All of this makes Mastodon a more open, calm network focused on user experience.

features.1: No algorithm: the feed is always chronological
features.2: Posts with text, images, videos and polls
features.3: You choose your instance (or can create your own)

mastodon.reasonToUse: Use Mastodon if you want a social network without engagement algorithms and with communities moderated by real people instead of corporations.

challenges.1: Running your own instance requires constant moderation attention to prevent spam, hate speech, and federation with problematic instances.
challenges.2: The Mastodon server consumes considerable RAM in production; instances with hundreds of users typically need at least 2–4 GB RAM and a dedicated PostgreSQL database.
challenges.3: The learning curve for new users is real: choosing an instance, understanding federation, and finding people across instances is not intuitive.

nextcloud.description: A Google Drive alternative that lets you save, share and sync files on your own server.
nextcloud.longDescription: Nextcloud is a complete platform for storing and accessing your files over the internet, like a private Google Drive. You can upload documents, photos and videos, organize folders, share links and sync everything across your devices. You can also enable extra features like calendar, contacts, chat, video calls and collaborative document editing. All on your own server, with full control and no dependence on third-party companies.

features.1: File storage and synchronization across devices
features.2: Sharing with public links or password-protected ones
features.3: Extra features like calendar, contacts, chat and document editing

nextcloud.reasonToUse: Choose Nextcloud if you want a complete Google Drive replacement with your data under your control, especially useful for teams or families who share files frequently.

challenges.1: Performance degrades significantly without proper cache configuration (Redis/Memcached) and database setup — the default SQLite installation is not recommended for real use.
challenges.2: Major version upgrades frequently break installed apps; keeping the plugin ecosystem updated requires periodic attention.
challenges.3: Syncing large files or bulky photo libraries can be slow and resource-intensive on the server.

vaultwarden.description: A Bitwarden-compatible password manager, lightweight and easy to self-host.
vaultwarden.longDescription: Vaultwarden is a lightweight alternative to Bitwarden, one of the most popular password managers. With it, you can store passwords, logins, secure notes and other information in encrypted form, accessing everything through the browser or the official Bitwarden app. The big advantage of Vaultwarden is that it uses minimal resources and is very easy to install, ideal for those who want to control their passwords on their own server.

features.1: Compatible with official Bitwarden apps
features.2: End-to-end encryption for passwords and sensitive data
features.3: Uses minimal resources and runs well even on simple servers

vaultwarden.reasonToUse: Use Vaultwarden if you want the full Bitwarden experience (including TOTP and organizations) without paying for a premium subscription, hosting everything on your own server with minimal resource usage.

challenges.1: Being an unofficial project, it receives no direct support from Bitwarden; compatibility updates with official clients depend on community response speed.
challenges.2: You are responsible for SQLite database backups — a failure without backup means total password loss.
challenges.3: Exposing the server on the internet requires mandatory HTTPS and attention to host security; it is not recommended without a properly configured reverse proxy.

passbolt.description: A password manager focused on collaborative use by teams and companies.
passbolt.longDescription: Passbolt is a password manager built especially for teams. It allows secure password sharing among team members, with permission control and activity logs. All passwords are end-to-end encrypted, and access is done through the browser using an extension. It's a good choice for companies or groups that need to manage credentials together, while still maintaining full control by running on your own server.

features.1: Secure password sharing among team members
features.2: Permission control and access logs
features.3: Web interface with private key authentication

passbolt.reasonToUse: Choose Passbolt if your team needs to share credentials in an auditable and encrypted way, with granular control over who accesses what — something individual password managers don't offer.

challenges.1: Installation requires PHP, MySQL/MariaDB and SMTP email configuration; the process is more involved than purely Docker-based tools.
challenges.2: Each user needs to install the browser extension and configure their GPG key, adding friction to team adoption.
challenges.3: Advanced features like LDAP/AD and detailed auditing are only available in the Business tier (paid).

element.description: A secure and decentralized messaging app based on the Matrix protocol.
element.longDescription: Element is a messaging app similar to WhatsApp or Telegram, but with a key difference: it uses the open Matrix protocol, which allows communication between different servers. You can create public or private rooms, send files, make voice and video calls, and even chat with people on other services (like Slack, Discord or Telegram via bridges). Element can be used on public servers or your own, offering full privacy and control over data.

features.1: Messages, files, voice and video calls
features.2: Create public or private rooms
features.3: Compatible with Telegram, Discord, Slack, etc. via bridges

element.reasonToUse: Use Element if you need a corporate or community messenger with real encryption, without depending on Meta or Microsoft servers, with the ability to integrate other services via bridges.

challenges.1: The Matrix server (Synapse) is resource-heavy: instances with active federation easily consume 1–2 GB of RAM and grow quickly in the database.
challenges.2: The user experience in Element is still less polished than WhatsApp or Telegram, especially for group calls and notifications.
challenges.3: Bridges with third-party services (WhatsApp, Telegram) require separate configuration and may break when those services update their APIs.

lemmy.description: A forum and communities platform, similar to Reddit, but decentralized and without tracking.
lemmy.longDescription: Lemmy is a network of themed community forums where anyone can post links, text and start discussions. It operates in a federated way, with multiple instances connecting to each other, like Mastodon. Each instance can have its own rules, but you can participate in communities from other instances without creating multiple accounts. It's a great Reddit alternative for those seeking more privacy and freedom.

features.1: Themed communities with upvote and downvote voting
features.2: Federation between instances via ActivityPub
features.3: Simple, lightweight interface with no trackers

lemmy.reasonToUse: Choose Lemmy if you want to create or participate in themed forums without paid recommendation algorithms, without data sold to advertisers, and with transparent community-driven moderation.

challenges.1: Federation between instances still has occasional failures — posts and comments from other instances may be delayed or fail to sync correctly.
challenges.2: The user base is still small compared to Reddit, meaning less active communities for many topics.
challenges.3: Running an instance requires active moderation to prevent spam and illegal content federated from other instances appearing in local feeds.

jitsimeet.description: A secure video conferencing tool with no registration required, ideal for quick meetings.
jitsimeet.longDescription: Jitsi Meet is a video call platform that works directly in the browser, with no installation or account needed. It's an alternative to Zoom and Google Meet, focused on privacy and ease of use. You can host your own instance for full control over meetings. It supports screen sharing, chat, meeting recording and password-protected rooms. Ideal for teams, classes and online meetings.

features.1: Video and audio calls directly in the browser
features.2: Screen sharing and integrated chat
features.3: Password-protected rooms, no mandatory registration

jitsimeet.reasonToUse: Use Jitsi Meet if you need quick, registration-free video conferences, or if you want to host your own meeting infrastructure to ensure no data passes through third-party servers.

challenges.1: Call quality in large groups (above 10–15 participants) degrades noticeably without a dedicated server with good bandwidth and CPU.
challenges.2: Installing Jitsi with the videobridge (JVB) is complex and requires open UDP ports, which can be problematic in environments with restrictive firewalls.
challenges.3: Features like cloud recording and automatic transcription require additional setup of components like Jibri, which has its own hardware requirements.

bookstack.description: A documentation and note-taking platform in a books-and-pages format, ideal for teams or personal use.
bookstack.longDescription: BookStack is a tool for organizing knowledge in a books, chapters and pages format. It can be used to create manuals, wikis, technical documentation or personal notes. The interface is simple and works directly in the browser, with support for user permissions, edit history and a visual editor. Being self-hosted, you have full control over the content and can adapt it for individual or team use.

features.1: Organization by books, chapters and pages
features.2: Visual editor with change history
features.3: Access control by users and permissions

bookstack.reasonToUse: Choose BookStack if you want a wiki or knowledge base with a clear structure (book → chapter → page) without the complexity of Confluence or MediaWiki.

challenges.1: The rigid book/chapter/page structure can be limiting for content that doesn't fit neatly into that hierarchy.
challenges.2: It has no native support for databases other than MySQL/MariaDB, which can complicate installations in environments that use PostgreSQL.
challenges.3: The internal search is functional but less powerful than dedicated tools — very large documentation sets can become difficult to navigate.

rsshub.description: An RSS feed generator for sites that don't natively support it, ideal for following everything in one place.
rsshub.longDescription: RSSHub allows you to create RSS feeds for virtually any website, even those that don't offer this feature. With it, you can follow updates from social networks, blogs, forums, online stores and more, all in an RSS reader. It's a great tool for centralizing news and avoiding the use of social media to stay informed. Being self-hosted, you have control over which sources to follow and can avoid trackers.

features.1: Creates RSS feeds for sites that don't have native support
features.2: Works with social networks, e-commerce, blogs and forums
features.3: Allows customizing and filtering received content

rsshub.reasonToUse: Use RSSHub if you want to follow content from platforms that don't offer RSS (like Twitter/X, Reddit or YouTube) without having to open each site or app individually.

challenges.1: Many routes depend on unofficial APIs that break without warning when sites change their structure or block scrapers.
challenges.2: Hosting a public instance can attract abusive use and IP blocks from the monitored sites.
challenges.3: Documentation is extensive but inconsistent — configuring less popular routes may require reading source code.

discourse.description: A modern forum platform with social login support, notifications and a chat-app-like interface.
discourse.longDescription: Discourse is a forum tool with a modern look and current features. It can be used to create communities, support groups, internal discussion areas and more. It supports social login, email notifications, a mention system and category organization. It's also responsive, working well on mobile. Being self-hosted, you can configure it your way, with your own rules and full control over the user base.

features.1: Forums organized by categories with a reputation system
features.2: Modern interface with mentions, markdown and notification support
features.3: Works well on mobile and allows social login (optional)

discourse.reasonToUse: Choose Discourse if you need an online community with robust moderation, a reputation system and email notifications — it's the gold standard for support forums and project communities.

challenges.1: The official installation mandates Docker and a server with at least 2 GB of RAM; installations on cheap VPS frequently suffer from slowness.
challenges.2: RAM consumption grows with the number of active users and plugins, making operating costs higher than other alternatives.
challenges.3: Updates are frequent and sometimes break plugins or theme customizations, requiring regular maintenance.

pixelfed.description: A social network for sharing photos, similar to Instagram.
pixelfed.longDescription: Pixelfed is a social network for photos and short videos, designed for those who want to share images with privacy and control. It has a look similar to Instagram, with a feed, likes and comments, but focused on privacy: no recommendation algorithms and no ads. Like Mastodon, Pixelfed operates across multiple instances that communicate with each other. You can follow and be followed by people from other Fediverse instances, all open source and without depending on centralized companies.

features.1: Photo feed with likes, comments and stories
features.2: No ads, trackers or recommendation algorithm
features.3: Operates across multiple instances that connect with each other

pixelfed.reasonToUse: Use Pixelfed if you want to share photos without having your images used to train advertising algorithms or sold to advertisers, maintaining control over your gallery and audience.

challenges.1: The project is less mature than Mastodon — updates can introduce bugs and ActivityPub interoperability with other platforms has inconsistencies.
challenges.2: Installation requires PHP, Redis, MySQL and a queue server; it's more complex than other Fediverse platforms.
challenges.3: The community is still small, meaning less interaction and content discovery compared to Instagram.

signal.description: A privacy-focused messenger with end-to-end encryption and no tracking.
signal.longDescription: Signal is a messaging app that prioritizes security and privacy. All conversations are protected by end-to-end encryption, and no data is stored beyond what is necessary. You can exchange messages, make voice and video calls, create groups and send files. The official app uses the organization's own servers, but it is also possible to host your own Signal server for full control over data. It's used by people and organizations that value confidentiality.

features.1: End-to-end encryption by default
features.2: Secure voice and video calls
features.3: No ads, tracking or personal data collection

signal.reasonToUse: Use Signal if you need a messenger with audited and proven encryption for sensitive conversations — it's the choice of journalists, activists and security professionals.

challenges.1: Self-hosting the Signal server is extremely complex and receives no official support — most users depend on Signal Foundation's servers.
challenges.2: Requires a phone number for registration, which reduces anonymity and excludes people without access to a verifiable number.
challenges.3: It is not federated — all users must be on the same server (signal.org), creating a centralized point even with strong encryption.

stirling-pdf.description: A complete tool for editing and converting PDF files in the browser, directly from your server.
stirling-pdf.longDescription: Stirling-PDF is a web application that allows you to perform various operations on PDF files, such as merging, splitting, rotating, converting, password-protecting, extracting text and much more. Everything works directly in the browser, without needing to send your files to external services. It's ideal for those who want a practical, local and secure solution for handling PDFs daily, whether for work or personal use.

features.1: Merge, split, rotate and convert PDF files
features.2: Password-protect, extract text and images
features.3: Everything works in the browser, no cloud upload required

stirling-pdf.reasonToUse: Use Stirling-PDF if you handle sensitive PDFs and don't want to upload them to online services like iLovePDF or SmallPDF — processing everything locally ensures your documents never leave your server.

challenges.1: Some advanced operations (OCR, Office-to-PDF conversion) require extra dependencies like LibreOffice and Tesseract installed on the server.
challenges.2: The English-only interface may be a barrier for less technical users unfamiliar with tools in other languages.
challenges.3: It does not support editing content inside a PDF — it's a file manipulation tool, not a text editor.

activitypub.description: An open protocol that allows different platforms to communicate with each other in the Fediverse.
activitypub.longDescription: ActivityPub is a communication protocol used to connect different federated platforms. It allows a person on one site (like Mastodon) to interact with another on a different service (like PeerTube or Pixelfed), as if they were on the same network. Each server can have its own rules and focus, but still talk to others. It's the foundation of what we call the Fediverse — a set of free services that work together.

features.1: Enables federation between different services
features.2: Foundation of networks like Mastodon, PeerTube, Pixelfed and Lemmy
features.3: Developed as an open standard by the W3C

activitypub.reasonToUse: Understanding ActivityPub is essential for anyone wanting to build their own federated platform or contribute to the Fediverse ecosystem — it's the protocol that makes interoperability between dozens of distinct services possible.

challenges.1: The specification has ambiguous parts, leading to incompatible implementations between platforms — a Mastodon post doesn't always appear the same way in Pixelfed or PeerTube.
challenges.2: Implementing ActivityPub correctly requires handling HTTP signatures, delivery queues and WebFinger resolution, with a steep learning curve.
challenges.3: There is no abuse control mechanism between instances — a malicious instance can federate unwanted content until manually blocked.

matrix.description: An open real-time communication protocol used by apps like Element.
matrix.longDescription: Matrix is a protocol that enables the exchange of messages, calls and files between different servers. It was designed to be secure, decentralized and interoperable. With it, you can use an app like Element to communicate with people on other Matrix servers, or even integrate with services like Slack and Telegram via bridges. It's a solid foundation for those who want full control over their online communication.

features.1: Messages, calls and file sharing
features.2: Works across different servers (federation)
features.3: Integration with other services via bridges

matrix.reasonToUse: Use Matrix if you need a decentralized communication protocol with end-to-end encryption and the ability to integrate multiple services in one place via bridges.

challenges.1: The reference server (Synapse) is known for consuming a lot of RAM and CPU, especially with active federation — a small instance may need 2–4 GB of RAM.
challenges.2: The Synapse database grows quickly with message history and may need periodic maintenance to avoid performance issues.
challenges.3: The user experience varies greatly between clients — Element has improved, but still falls behind mature messengers in usability.

xmpp.description: An open instant messaging protocol, used for decades in free and federated services.
xmpp.longDescription: XMPP (Extensible Messaging and Presence Protocol) is a messaging protocol created in 1999, long before modern messengers. It allows exchanging messages, files and even making calls, all in a decentralized way. There are many XMPP servers and clients, and you can freely choose who to connect with. Despite being old, it continues to be maintained, with a focus on simplicity, privacy and freedom.

features.1: Instant messages and presence (online/offline)
features.2: Fully federated, with various available clients and servers
features.3: Simple, lightweight and ideal for use on diverse devices
features.4: It's the protocol used by WhatsApp

xmpp.reasonToUse: Use XMPP if you want a lightweight, federated messaging protocol with decades of stability, especially useful in corporate environments with existing infrastructure or legacy system integration needs.

challenges.1: Extension fragmentation (XEPs) means different clients support different features — OMEMO encrypted messages, for example, don't work equally in all apps.
challenges.2: The user experience in modern clients still falls below WhatsApp or Telegram, making adoption difficult for less technical groups.
challenges.3: Setting up an XMPP server with all modern features (video calls, multi-device sync) requires installing and configuring multiple modules.

email.description: The classic digital communication protocol, decentralized by nature and broadly compatible.
email.longDescription: Email is one of the oldest and most universal forms of communication on the internet. It works based on open protocols like SMTP, IMAP and POP3, which allow different providers and servers to exchange messages with each other. Unlike social networks and modern messengers, email was born decentralized: anyone can host their own server and have complete control over their messages. It's compatible with virtually all systems and devices.

features.1: Based on open protocols
features.2: Fully decentralized: anyone can run their own server
features.3: Works everywhere, with thousands of compatible apps and clients

email.reasonToUse: Consider self-hosting email if you want full sovereignty over your communications and don't want to depend on providers like Google or Microsoft — especially relevant for organizations with sensitive data.

challenges.1: Maintaining good deliverability is hard: new servers frequently have emails marked as spam by large providers until they build a reputation.
challenges.2: Correctly configuring SPF, DKIM and DMARC requires technical knowledge — mistakes leave the server vulnerable to spoofing or rejected emails.
challenges.3: Ongoing maintenance is demanding: security updates, blacklist monitoring and TLS certificate management require constant attention.

ghost.description: A lightweight platform for blogs and websites, with a modern editor and a focus on performance.
ghost.longDescription: Ghost is a publishing platform designed for those who want to create blogs, personal sites or project pages with a focus on simplicity and speed. It has a modern editor, theme support, native SEO and even newsletter integration. Being self-hosted, you have full control over the content and site's appearance.

features.1: Modern editor with Markdown and block support
features.2: Clean look with customizable themes
features.3: Great performance, native SEO and newsletter integration

ghost.reasonToUse: Choose Ghost if you want a fast, focused publishing platform without WordPress's complexity — ideal for blogs, newsletters and content portals that prioritize clean reading experiences.

challenges.1: Requires Node.js to run, which can complicate installation on traditional shared hosting — most users opt for VPS or Docker.
challenges.2: The theme and integration ecosystem is much smaller than WordPress, limiting more elaborate customizations.
challenges.3: Major version upgrades sometimes require manual database migration and can break custom themes.

misskey.description: A fun and customizable social network, with posts, reactions and a federated timeline.
misskey.longDescription: Misskey is a decentralized social network similar to Mastodon, but with more focus on customization and interactions. It allows posting text, images, videos and even 'notes' with varied styles. It supports reactions (like custom emojis), widgets, themes and other features that make the experience lighter and more fun. Like other Fediverse platforms, Misskey operates through federated instances using the ActivityPub protocol.

features.1: Posts with reactions, emojis and interactive widgets
features.2: Highly customizable with theme and extension support
features.3: Federated communication with other Fediverse networks
features.4: Developed and widely used in Japan

misskey.reasonToUse: Use Misskey if you want a more playful and customizable social experience than Mastodon, with custom emojis, varied reactions and a more expressive interface.

challenges.1: Resource consumption is higher than Pleroma or Mastodon — instances need at least 1 GB of RAM and a well-configured PostgreSQL database.
challenges.2: English documentation is limited; most support material is in Japanese, which can make troubleshooting difficult.
challenges.3: The project has several active forks (Calckey, Firefish, Catodon) with different maintenance states, making the choice of the right version confusing.

rocketchat.description: A team messaging platform similar to Slack, but with full data control.
rocketchat.longDescription: Rocket.Chat is a messaging app focused on team communication, ideal for companies, working groups and communities. It allows real-time messaging, creating public or private channels, voice and video calls, file sharing and integration with other tools. The big advantage is that you can run everything on your own server, ensuring privacy and security without depending on external services.

features.1: Channels, direct messages, voice and video calls
features.2: User control, permissions and integrations
features.3: Modern interface with desktop and mobile apps

rocketchat.reasonToUse: Choose Rocket.Chat if your organization needs a self-hosted Slack with omnichannel support (customer service via chat, email and social networks in one place).

challenges.1: RAM consumption is high — an instance with few users can already require 2 GB, and larger instances need dedicated infrastructure.
challenges.2: Frequent updates can break integrations or installed apps, requiring testing before applying to production.
challenges.3: Advanced features like omnichannel and app marketplace require an Enterprise subscription, which may surprise those expecting everything to be free.

plane.description: A task and project management tool with a modern look, focused on self-organized teams.
plane.longDescription: Plane is an open source app for organizing tasks, bugs, ideas and team projects. It has a look similar to Linear or Jira, with Kanban boards, custom statuses, priorities and labels. It's ideal for development teams, but also works well for any group that wants to track task progress more clearly. It can be self-hosted to ensure full control over team data.

features.1: Kanban boards, custom statuses and priorities
features.2: Organization by projects, sprints, cycles or teams
features.3: Fast, modern and easy-to-use interface

plane.reasonToUse: Use Plane if you want a Jira alternative with a cleaner interface and no high licensing costs — it's especially suited for development teams that already use agile methodologies.

challenges.1: The project is still in active development and some advanced features (automations, detailed reports) are incomplete or unstable.
challenges.2: Docker installation requires setting up several services together (backend, frontend, worker, Redis, PostgreSQL), which can be cumbersome for those without Docker Compose experience.
challenges.3: Integrations with external tools (GitHub, Slack, GitLab) are still limited compared to Jira or Linear.

ipfs.description: A protocol for storing and sharing files in a distributed way, without relying on central servers.
ipfs.longDescription: IPFS (InterPlanetary File System) is a protocol that allows storing and accessing files in a decentralized way, similar to how torrents work. Instead of relying on a fixed server, files are distributed across computers in the network. This makes access more resistant to failures, censorship and deletions. It's used for websites, images, videos and other content that needs to remain available more securely and permanently. Note that IPFS alone does not replace an indexing or naming system — for that, IPNS or other additional layers are used.

features.1: File storage and access without central servers
features.2: Content is identified by its hash, not a fixed address
features.3: Resistant to censorship and server failures

ipfs.reasonToUse: Use IPFS if you need to publish content in a censorship-resistant and deletion-proof way, or if you want to explore distributed storage for decentralized applications (dApps) and immutable files.

challenges.1: Files are not permanent by default — if no node is pinning the content, it can disappear from the network.
challenges.2: Access speed is unpredictable and generally slower than traditional HTTP, especially for rare content with few seeders.
challenges.3: Without a dedicated pinning service (like Pinata or Infura), keeping content reliably accessible requires always-online infrastructure of your own.

bitwarden.description: A secure and complete password manager, with official apps and a self-hosting option.
bitwarden.longDescription: Bitwarden is a password manager that lets you save logins, secure notes, cards and other data in encrypted form. It can be accessed via browser, mobile or desktop app, and also offers autofill and strong password generation. It's a reliable alternative to paid password managers and, unlike most, can be self-hosted for those who want full control over their data.

features.1: End-to-end encryption for passwords and sensitive data
features.2: Apps for browser, mobile and desktop
features.3: Can be used on the official cloud or on your own server

bitwarden.reasonToUse: Choose official Bitwarden (not Vaultwarden) if you want professional support, security updates guaranteed by the company's team, and compliance with corporate standards like SOC 2.

challenges.1: Self-hosting the official Bitwarden server is heavier than Vaultwarden — it requires Docker with multiple containers and at least 2 GB of RAM.
challenges.2: The free cloud tier has limitations (no integrated TOTP, no advanced reports), leading many users to Vaultwarden as an alternative.
challenges.3: Keeping the self-hosted server updated is critical for security — vulnerability patches need to be applied quickly.

diaspora.description: A decentralized social network focused on privacy, with no ads or tracking.
diaspora.longDescription: Diaspora* is a social network that operates through 'pods', independent servers that connect with each other. You can choose a pod to create your account or host your own. The system allows following people, publishing text, images, liking and commenting, all focused on user control and privacy. It was one of the first projects to propose a free alternative to centralized social networks like Facebook. Unlike Mastodon or Friendica, the protocol used by Diaspora* is not compatible with ActivityPub.

features.1: Decentralized network focused on privacy
features.2: Posts with text, links and images
features.3: Control over who can see each post (aspects)

diaspora.reasonToUse: Use Diaspora* if you want a decentralized social network with a privacy system based on 'aspects' — contact groups that determine who sees each post.

challenges.1: The project's development has slowed significantly in recent years — updates are infrequent and the maintainer community is small.
challenges.2: It is not compatible with ActivityPub, isolating users from the broader Fediverse ecosystem (Mastodon, Pixelfed, etc.).
challenges.3: Installation requires Ruby on Rails, PostgreSQL and various dependencies — it's one of the most labor-intensive platforms to self-host in the Fediverse.

jellyfin.description: A media server to organize and watch your movies, series and music anywhere.
jellyfin.longDescription: Jellyfin is a personal streaming platform that lets you organize and access your collection of movies, series, music and photos from any device. It works like a 'home Netflix', with a beautiful interface and apps for TV, mobile and browser. Everything is hosted by you, with no tracking, no subscription and full control over your files. It's a great option for those who want to set up their own media server.

features.1: Streaming of movies, series, music and photos
features.2: Modern interface with apps for web, mobile and TV
features.3: Completely free, with no trackers or artificial limits

jellyfin.reasonToUse: Choose Jellyfin if you want a completely free media server with no proprietary components or cloud account dependency — unlike Plex, it works 100% offline.

challenges.1: Real-time video transcoding (for devices that don't support the original codec) requires adequate CPU or GPU — modest servers may lag on 4K files.
challenges.2: Hardware acceleration for transcoding (Intel QuickSync, NVIDIA NVENC) requires manual configuration and can be unstable in some environments.
challenges.3: Smart TV and console apps are less polished than Plex's and may have bugs on certain TV models.

zulip.description: A team messaging app that combines chat style with forum organization.
zulip.longDescription: Zulip is a team messaging app that organizes conversations by topics within channels, making it easier to follow multiple discussions at once without getting lost. It's ideal for teams that work asynchronously, where not everyone responds right away. It offers real-time messages, file support, emojis, integrations with other tools and can be self-hosted for those seeking full control over communication.

features.1: Conversations organized by topics within channels
features.2: Ideal for teams that work asynchronously
features.3: Apps for desktop, mobile and browser

zulip.reasonToUse: Use Zulip if your team struggles with the chaos of real-time chats — the topic model within streams allows following multiple parallel discussions without losing context.

challenges.1: Self-hosted installation requires Ubuntu 20.04 or 22.04 (no official support for other systems) and the process uses an installation script that makes many system changes.
challenges.2: The topic model requires team discipline to work well — without everyone committing to naming topics correctly, the organization falls apart.
challenges.3: The interface, while functional, has a steeper learning curve than Slack or Mattermost for new users.

osticket.description: A ticket support system, ideal for organizing technical or customer service requests.
osticket.longDescription: osTicket is a helpdesk platform that allows receiving, organizing and responding to tickets efficiently. It's used by technical support teams, customer service or internal company departments. With it, you can centralize and track all help requests, with history, priorities and agent assignment. It's fully self-hosted and can be customized according to team needs.

features.1: Receiving and organizing tickets via email or form
features.2: Tracking by status, priority and responsible agent
features.3: Complete conversation history and field customization

osticket.reasonToUse: Choose osTicket if you need a simple and stable ticket support system without paying for Zendesk or Freshdesk licenses — it works well for teams with up to a few dozen agents.

challenges.1: The admin interface is dated and less intuitive than modern solutions like Freshdesk, which may increase training time for new agents.
challenges.2: Lack of native advanced automation features — routing rules and SLA management are basic compared to commercial platforms.
challenges.3: Integrations with modern tools (Slack, Teams, CRMs) don't exist natively and require custom development or third-party plugins.

friendica.description: A federated social network focused on compatibility and integration with other networks.
friendica.longDescription: Friendica is a decentralized social network that lets you post text, images, like, comment and interact with people from other networks like Mastodon, Diaspora* and even email accounts. One of its biggest differentiators is compatibility with multiple protocols, facilitating connections between different platforms. It has a simpler look reminiscent of classic social networks, focused on accessibility and integration.

features.1: Compatible with various Fediverse networks and even email
features.2: Posts with text, links, images and videos
features.3: Allows creating groups, forums and contact lists

friendica.reasonToUse: Use Friendica if you want to participate in the Fediverse and Diaspora* ecosystem simultaneously, or if you need longer and more structured posts than Mastodon allows by default.

challenges.1: The interface is visually outdated and can be confusing for new users coming from modern social networks.
challenges.2: Installation requires PHP, MySQL and background worker configuration — not as simple as other Docker-based platforms.
challenges.3: Development is done by a small team, meaning less frequent updates and slower bug fixes.

pleroma.description: A lightweight and customizable federated social network, ideal for small servers.
pleroma.longDescription: Pleroma is a microblogging platform like Mastodon, but with a focus on lightness and performance. It's ideal for those who want to run their own instance on servers with limited resources. It supports the ActivityPub protocol, allowing interaction with users from other networks like Mastodon, Misskey and Pixelfed. Its interface can be customized, and it's also possible to use other community-built interfaces.

features.1: Lightweight and ideal for running on servers with limited resources
features.2: Compatible with other Fediverse networks via ActivityPub
features.3: Customizable interface with theme support

pleroma.reasonToUse: Use Pleroma if you want to run a personal Fediverse instance on limited hardware — it works well on a 512 MB RAM VPS, something impossible with Mastodon.

challenges.1: The main project has slow development; many users have migrated to the Akkoma fork, which is more active but has fragmented documentation.
challenges.2: The Pleroma community has a history of moderation controversies, which has led some Fediverse instances to avoid federating with Pleroma servers.
challenges.3: Initial configuration in Elixir/Erlang can be challenging for administrators without experience in those languages.

hubzilla.description: A federated social network focused on portable identity and multiple features in one place.
hubzilla.longDescription: Hubzilla is a federated platform that goes beyond a common social network. In addition to posts and social interactions, it allows creating blogs, wikis, forums and even personal pages. Its biggest differentiator is the portable identity feature: you can move between servers (hubs) without losing your profile or data. This offers a much higher level of autonomy within the Fediverse.

features.1: Portable identity between servers (Zot protocol)
features.2: Posts, forums, blogs and wikis all in one place
features.3: Advanced permission and privacy control

hubzilla.reasonToUse: Use Hubzilla if you need portable identity between servers — the Zot protocol allows cloning your profile across multiple hubs, ensuring availability even if one server goes offline.

challenges.1: It's one of the most complex systems in the Fediverse — the interface and configuration have a steep learning curve even for technical users.
challenges.2: Installation requires PHP, Apache/Nginx, MySQL and various dependencies, with no well-documented official Docker path.
challenges.3: The user base is small, meaning limited support and less active communities for most topics.

mattermost.description: A team messaging platform similar to Slack, focused on privacy and integration.
mattermost.longDescription: Mattermost is a team messaging app that allows real-time communication via channels, direct messages and integrations with other tools. It's ideal for companies, technical teams and organizations that want full control over their communications. It can be self-hosted and has features like file sharing, video calls via plugin, webhook support and integration with Git, CI/CD and more.

features.1: Messages via channels, groups and private conversations
features.2: Integrations with tools like GitLab, Jira and CI/CD
features.3: Focus on security, control and corporate use

mattermost.reasonToUse: Choose Mattermost if your development team needs a self-hosted Slack with native integration to GitLab, GitHub and CI/CD pipelines — it's the most complete for DevOps workflows.

challenges.1: The free tier has significant limitations — message retention, advanced integrations and compliance require the Enterprise plan, which has a per-user cost.
challenges.2: Voice and video calls depend on external plugins, unlike Slack where it's natively integrated.
challenges.3: In instances with many channels and users, performance can degrade without careful database and cache configuration.

bigbluebutton.description: A video conferencing platform focused on online teaching, with features like whiteboards and virtual classrooms.
bigbluebutton.longDescription: BigBlueButton is a video conferencing tool designed for distance education. It allows creating virtual classrooms with video, audio, chat, presentations, collaborative whiteboard and even polls. Teachers can record classes, split participants into smaller groups and track interaction in real time. Everything can be hosted on your own server, ensuring control and privacy.

features.1: Video conferencing with whiteboard and screen sharing
features.2: Classrooms with chat, polls and working groups
features.3: Recording and moderation features for online teaching

bigbluebutton.reasonToUse: Choose BigBlueButton if you manage a distance learning platform and need pedagogical features (whiteboard, breakout rooms, polls) natively integrated, without paying for Zoom or Google Meet.

challenges.1: Server requirements are high: the official installation requires 64-bit Ubuntu with at least 8 CPU cores and 16 GB of RAM to support classes of 50+ students.
challenges.2: Installation is complex and only supported on Ubuntu — there is no stable official Docker image for production.
challenges.3: Recording performance and post-class video processing can be slow and consume a lot of disk space.

gitlab.description: A complete collaborative development platform, with code management, CI/CD and project management.
gitlab.longDescription: GitLab is an all-in-one tool for development teams. In addition to version control with Git, it offers private repositories, issue management, merge requests, CI/CD, wiki and permission-based access control. It can be used on the official cloud or hosted by you, ensuring full control over your projects and data. It's a robust GitHub alternative with a focus on privacy and collaboration.

features.1: Git repositories with access control and history
features.2: Native CI/CD integration and automated pipelines
features.3: Issue management, merge requests, wikis and more

gitlab.reasonToUse: Choose self-hosted GitLab if your team needs integrated CI/CD, project management and private repositories on a single platform without depending on GitHub or paying for corporate plans.

challenges.1: GitLab is the heaviest software on this list — it requires at least 4 GB of RAM (8 GB recommended) and can be slow on modest servers.
challenges.2: Updates must follow sequential version order; skipping versions can corrupt the database and requires manual recovery procedures.
challenges.3: Storage consumption grows quickly with repositories, CI artifacts and Docker images — disk planning is essential.

gitea.description: A lightweight code hosting platform, with a simple interface and ideal for personal servers.
gitea.longDescription: Gitea is a lightweight and fast alternative to GitHub and GitLab, perfect for those who want to host their own Git repositories. It offers essential features like issues, wiki, pull requests, CI via integration, access control and a friendly interface. Being very lightweight, it works well even on modest servers, making it ideal for small teams, personal projects or organizations that want more control with less complexity.

features.1: Git repository hosting with web interface
features.2: Support for issues, wikis, pull requests and permissions
features.3: Lightweight and easy to install, ideal for small servers

gitea.reasonToUse: Use Gitea if you want to host Git repositories on a resource-limited server — it works well with under 512 MB of RAM and is much simpler to install and maintain than GitLab.

challenges.1: The native CI/CD (Gitea Actions) is still in development and less mature than GitLab CI or GitHub Actions — teams with complex pipelines will need external tools.
challenges.2: The project went through a controversial fork that created Forgejo; part of the community has migrated, and choosing between the two can be confusing.
challenges.3: Corporate features like advanced LDAP, auditing and compliance are less robust than in GitLab or Bitbucket.

openproject.description: A complete project and task management tool, ideal for organized teams.
openproject.longDescription: OpenProject is a project management platform that allows efficient planning, tracking and collaboration on tasks. It supports Gantt charts, agile boards, time tracking, wikis and much more. It can be used by teams of any size, with permission control and customization. It's a great Jira and Trello alternative, especially for those who prefer a self-hosted solution.

features.1: Task management with Gantt charts, lists and agile boards
features.2: Time tracking, wikis and team collaboration
features.3: Modern web interface with per-project permission control

openproject.reasonToUse: Choose OpenProject if you need project management with real Gantt charts, time tracking and roadmaps — features that Trello and Plane don't offer natively.

challenges.1: Installation requires Ruby on Rails and PostgreSQL, with official support only for DEB/RPM packages or Docker — alternative setups are not supported.
challenges.2: Features like GitLab/GitHub integration and budget management are only available on paid tiers (Basic and above).
challenges.3: The interface has many options and can seem overly complex for small teams that only need a simple Kanban board.

uptimekuma.description: A site and service monitor that alerts you when something goes down, with a beautiful and easy-to-use dashboard.
uptimekuma.longDescription: Uptime Kuma is a tool for monitoring whether your websites, servers or services are online. It periodically checks the addresses you configure and sends alerts if any of them go down. You can receive notifications via email, Telegram, Discord and more. The dashboard is simple, beautiful and shows uptime with graphs and history. All of this can be hosted on your own server, without depending on external services.

features.1: Monitoring of websites, servers and services with alerts
features.2: Notifications via email, Telegram, Discord, Webhook and more
features.3: Dashboard with history, graphs and real-time status

uptimekuma.reasonToUse: Use Uptime Kuma if you need to monitor your self-hosted services and receive immediate alerts when something goes down — it's the simplest and most beautiful tool for this purpose, without needing to configure Zabbix or Nagios.

challenges.1: It has no support for complete infrastructure monitoring (CPU, RAM, disk) — it's focused on endpoint availability, not system metrics.
challenges.2: The default SQLite database can have performance issues with many monitors and long history — large instances need to migrate to MySQL.
challenges.3: It has no on-call scheduling or alert escalation features — for larger operations, tools like PagerDuty or OpsGenie are necessary.

plex.description: A media server to organize and watch your movies, series and music with a professional look.
plex.longDescription: Plex is a personal streaming platform that lets you organize your collection of movies, series, music and photos, and access them from anywhere. It has a polished interface, apps for mobile, smart TVs and browser. Although it has paid features and works with a cloud account, it can also be self-hosted for local use. It's ideal for those seeking a Netflix-like experience with their own library.

features.1: Personal media streaming with modern interface
features.2: Apps for mobile, browser, smart TVs and consoles
features.3: Works locally or with remote access via Plex account

plex.reasonToUse: Choose Plex if you want the best compatibility with smart TVs and consoles — official Plex apps are available on more platforms than Jellyfin, with a more polished experience.

challenges.1: Requires a mandatory account on Plex's servers, even for exclusively local use — this means your usage metadata passes through the company.
challenges.2: Important features like remote access and offline sync require a paid Plex Pass subscription.
challenges.3: The code is not fully open — you depend on the company to keep the apps and servers running, with no continuity guarantee.

languagetool.description: A grammar and spell-checking tool with support for many languages, including Portuguese.
languagetool.longDescription: LanguageTool is a smart text corrector that helps review spelling, grammar and writing style. It works in Portuguese and many other languages, and can be used as a browser extension, online editor or integrated into apps like LibreOffice. For those who prefer not to depend on the cloud, it's possible to host your own instance and keep corrections local and private.

features.1: Spelling, grammar and text style correction
features.2: Support for many languages including Brazilian Portuguese
features.3: Browser extensions, LibreOffice integration and API

languagetool.reasonToUse: Use self-hosted LanguageTool if you work with sensitive documents and don't want to send text to Grammarly's or similar servers — processing locally guarantees full privacy.

challenges.1: The Java server consumes a lot of memory — for simultaneous multi-language support, at least 4–8 GB of RAM is recommended.
challenges.2: Suggestion quality in Portuguese is still below English — complex agreement errors and Brazilian regionalisms are less well handled.
challenges.3: More advanced style and clarity checks (like Grammarly Premium) are only available in the paid version of the online service.

owncast.description: A self-hosted live streaming platform, ideal for creators who want independence.
owncast.longDescription: Owncast lets you broadcast live directly from your server, without depending on centralized platforms like Twitch or YouTube. You can use common streaming software (like OBS) and broadcast to your own site, with live chat and visual customization. It's a great option for those who want more control, privacy and freedom over transmitted content.

features.1: Live streaming with integrated chat and visual customization
features.2: Compatible with OBS and other broadcasting tools
features.3: Runs on your own server, with no ads or tracking

owncast.reasonToUse: Use Owncast if you want to live stream without depending on Twitch's or YouTube's algorithms and policies, maintaining full control over your audience and content.

challenges.1: Live broadcasts require generous upload bandwidth on the server — 1080p60 streams can consume 6–10 Mbps of constant upload.
challenges.2: Transcoding multiple quality levels (for viewers with slow connections) requires considerable CPU — without transcoding, viewers with limited internet may have issues.
challenges.3: There is no discovery system — your audience needs to know your server address; there's no centralized platform for new viewers to find you.

azuracast.description: A complete system for creating and managing an online radio station, with streaming, scheduling and statistics.
azuracast.longDescription: AzuraCast is a self-hosted platform that lets you broadcast audio live or on schedule like an online radio station. It includes everything you need: control panel, music management, playlists, broadcast scheduling, listener statistics and live streaming support. It's ideal for community radio stations, independent projects or anyone who wants to explore the world of internet audio broadcasting with full control.

features.1: Station, playlist and live broadcast management
features.2: Automatic programming scheduling based on time slots
features.3: Complete dashboard with audience statistics and logs

azuracast.reasonToUse: Choose AzuraCast if you want to create a community or independent online radio station with scheduling automation, without paying for monthly plans from services like Radio.co.

challenges.1: Installation uses Docker and a proprietary script that takes full control of the server — not recommended to install on a server already hosting other services without proper isolation.
challenges.2: Broadcasts with many simultaneous listeners require significant bandwidth — each listener at 128 kbps consumes about 16 KB/s of upload.
challenges.3: AzuraCast updates can be unstable on long-running installations; migrations between major versions sometimes require manual reconfiguration.

keycloak.description: A centralized login system with single sign-on (SSO), permissions and service integration.
keycloak.longDescription: Keycloak is an identity and access management (IAM) tool that lets you centralize logins for multiple systems in one place. With it, you can offer single sign-on (SSO), social network authentication, two-factor auth (2FA) and permission control by group or role. It's ideal for teams, companies or projects that use multiple services and want to simplify access securely. Everything can be self-hosted without relying on external solutions.

features.1: Single sign-on (SSO) for multiple systems
features.2: Authentication with social networks, LDAP, 2FA and more
features.3: Complete user, group and permission management dashboard

keycloak.reasonToUse: Use Keycloak if you manage multiple self-hosted services and want users to log in once to access everything — it's the open source industry standard for corporate SSO.

challenges.1: Keycloak is memory-heavy — the official recommendation is at least 512 MB of Java heap, and in production with many users it can require 2–4 GB.
challenges.2: The admin interface is complex; configuring authentication flows, token scopes and SAML integrations requires specialized knowledge.
challenges.3: Major version upgrades frequently introduce incompatible configuration changes, requiring manual realm migration and integration review.

wordpress.description: A popular platform for creating websites and blogs, with thousands of plugins and themes available.
wordpress.longDescription: WordPress is one of the most widely used tools in the world for creating websites, blogs and online stores. With it, you can build anything from a simple page to a complete portal, using ready-made themes and plugins to add features. Despite being offered as a service by companies like WordPress.com, it can also be self-hosted, giving you full control over content and customization.

features.1: Website and blog creation with themes and plugins
features.2: Intuitive admin panel with multi-language support
features.3: Large community and accessible documentation

wordpress.reasonToUse: Choose WordPress if you need the largest plugin and theme ecosystem available — no other platform has more options for e-commerce, SEO, forms and integrations.

challenges.1: WordPress sites are frequent targets of automated attacks — keeping plugins and themes updated is mandatory and abandoned plugins are compromise vectors.
challenges.2: Performance can degrade with many active plugins; without cache configured (Redis, Varnish), medium-sized sites can become slow.
challenges.3: Plugin proliferation can create conflicting dependencies and make updates difficult — 'plugin hell' is a real problem in complex installations.

atprotocol.description: A protocol created for decentralized social networks, but currently limited to use within Bluesky.
atprotocol.longDescription: AT Protocol was developed to be the technical foundation of Bluesky, a decentralized social network that emerged as a Twitter alternative. It promises identity control, data portability and interoperability. It's an open protocol, but currently its use is restricted to Bluesky's infrastructure, with no support for federation or third-party use.

features.1: Focus on decentralized social networks with portable identity
features.2: Developed alongside the Bluesky platform
features.3: Not yet widely adopted or implemented outside Bluesky

atprotocol.reasonToUse: Study AT Protocol if you're interested in building decentralized social applications with DNS-domain-based portable identity — it's a different and interesting approach compared to ActivityPub.

challenges.1: Running your own PDS (Personal Data Server) is possible but still experimental, and integration with the Bluesky network depends on the company's centralized infrastructure.
challenges.2: There is no real federation yet — all searches and feeds pass through Bluesky's central servers, contradicting the decentralization promise.
challenges.3: The protocol is still under active evolution; third-party implementations face API changes without notice and incomplete documentation.

bluesky.description: A social network based on the AT Protocol, with a decentralized proposal in development.
bluesky.longDescription: Bluesky is a social network that presents itself as a decentralized Twitter alternative. It operates on the AT Protocol, which offers identity portability and interoperability between servers. At the moment, however, it still depends on a central instance (bsky.social) and doesn't offer federation or self-hosting support.

features.1: Interface similar to Twitter, with posts, likes and reposts
features.2: Own protocol (AT Protocol), but with limited use
features.3: Decentralization promise not yet fulfilled

bluesky.reasonToUse: Use Bluesky if you want a Twitter/X alternative with a familiar interface and a custom feeds system — the timeline algorithms are public and customizable, unlike Twitter.

challenges.1: Decentralization is still incomplete — most users depend on bsky.social and the company's central infrastructure is a single point of failure.
challenges.2: Self-hosting a PDS and participating in the network with real independence requires advanced technical knowledge and the experience is still unstable.
challenges.3: Content moderation is less mature than Mastodon — blocking and filtering tools are evolving but still basic.

rss.description: An open protocol for following updates from websites and blogs, without depending on algorithms or social networks.
rss.longDescription: RSS is a simple protocol that lets you follow news from websites, blogs, podcasts and video channels in one place. You use an RSS reader to gather content in a feed format, cleanly, without ads, algorithms or trackers. It's one of the oldest and most reliable ways to follow web content, and continues to be used by those seeking more autonomy and fewer distractions.

features.1: Automatic updates from websites, blogs, podcasts and more
features.2: Works with any RSS reader, online or self-hosted
features.3: No algorithms, no tracking, just the content you choose

rss.reasonToUse: Use RSS if you want to follow dozens of content sources in one place, without opening each site individually and without being manipulated by algorithms that decide what you see.

challenges.1: Many modern sites have dropped RSS support — platforms like Instagram, TikTok and LinkedIn don't offer feeds, requiring tools like RSSHub to work around this.
challenges.2: The learning curve for setting up an RSS reader and organizing multiple feeds can be a barrier for less technical users.
challenges.3: Self-hosting an RSS reader (like FreshRSS or Miniflux) requires server maintenance, unlike using a free app like Feedly.

atom.description: An open feed format, similar to RSS, created to be more flexible and modern.
atom.longDescription: Atom is a feed format created as an alternative to RSS, offering more standardization and extensibility. Like RSS, it lets you follow updates from websites, blogs and other content directly in a feed reader. Although less common, many sites still support Atom, and most modern readers support both formats. It's another option for those who want to follow the web without depending on social networks.

features.1: Allows following website and blog updates in real time
features.2: Compatible with most RSS readers
features.3: More structured and extensible than RSS, with official standard (IETF)

atom.reasonToUse: Use Atom if you're developing a publishing platform and need a more standardized and extensible feed format than RSS — the IETF specification is more rigorous and less ambiguous.

challenges.1: Atom has lower adoption than RSS among modern websites and blogs, meaning not all sites offer Atom feeds.
challenges.2: For end users, the practical difference between RSS and Atom is irrelevant — the format choice is more relevant for developers.
challenges.3: Like RSS, it depends on sites publishing and maintaining updated feeds — sites that drop feed support can no longer be followed.

bittorrent.description: A decentralized protocol for sharing files between users, without relying on central servers.
bittorrent.longDescription: BitTorrent is a protocol used to distribute large files over the internet efficiently and in a decentralized way. Instead of downloading from a single server, files are split into parts and shared among all those who are downloading or have already downloaded. This reduces the cost for distributors and makes the system more resistant to censorship and failures. It's used in various contexts, from distributing free software to personal files.

features.1: Direct user-to-user sharing (peer-to-peer)
features.2: Decentralized and resistant to failures and censorship
features.3: Used to distribute software, videos, music and other files

bittorrent.reasonToUse: Use BitTorrent if you distribute large files (like software ISOs or datasets) and want to reduce server bandwidth costs — the more people download, the faster it gets for everyone.

challenges.1: Participants' IP addresses are exposed to all other peers in the swarm by default, which can be a privacy concern.
challenges.2: Centralized trackers are still common and can be taken down — although DHT and PEX allow tracker-less operation, not all clients support these mechanisms well.
challenges.3: On corporate networks and ISPs, BitTorrent traffic is frequently blocked or throttled, which may make it impractical.

nostr.description: An experimental protocol for decentralized social networks, based on public keys and relays.
nostr.longDescription: Nostr (Notes and Other Stuff Transmitted by Relays) is a protocol created for decentralized social networks, simple and resistant to censorship. It uses cryptographic keys to identify users and servers called relays to transmit messages. Anyone can run a relay or client, but the ecosystem is still in an experimental phase, with few mature applications and usability limitations. It's popular among decentralization and Bitcoin enthusiasts, but still far from broad adoption.

features.1: Based on public key cryptography (no email or password)
features.2: Messages are transmitted through public or private relays
features.3: Focus on censorship resistance and technical simplicity

nostr.reasonToUse: Use Nostr if you want a social network with no mandatory central server and cryptographic key-based identity — it's the most censorship-resistant option among the protocols on this list.

challenges.1: The user experience is still immature — losing the private key means permanent identity loss, with no recovery possible.
challenges.2: Spam and abusive content are difficult to moderate without compromising censorship resistance — public relays are frequently flooded.
challenges.3: Content and people discovery is limited — there's no recommendation algorithm and finding users outside your circle is difficult.

tor.description: An anonymous network that protects your identity and allows accessing .onion sites outside the traditional internet.
tor.longDescription: Tor (The Onion Router) is a protocol that allows browsing the internet anonymously, hiding your IP address and location. It works by routing traffic through a distributed network of volunteer servers, making tracking and surveillance difficult. It also allows accessing hidden services with .onion addresses, which are not available on the conventional web. Tor is used by journalists, activists, researchers and anyone who wants more privacy online.

features.1: Anonymous browsing and protection against tracking
features.2: Access to .onion services and sites outside the regular web
features.3: Works with the Tor Browser and can be integrated into apps and servers

tor.reasonToUse: Use Tor if you need real anonymity on the internet — it's the standard tool for journalists, activists and researchers who need to protect their identity against surveillance.

challenges.1: Browsing speed is significantly lower than a direct connection — routing through multiple nodes adds considerable latency.
challenges.2: Some websites actively block connections from Tor exit nodes, requiring bridges to work around this.
challenges.3: Running a Tor exit node can attract legal attention, as third-party traffic passes through your IP — running only middle nodes is safer.

grafana.description: A tool for creating dashboards and visualizing metrics from systems, servers and applications.
grafana.longDescription: Grafana is a data visualization platform used to monitor servers, applications, databases and any other metrics source. It lets you create interactive dashboards with graphs, alerts and custom queries. It can be integrated with Prometheus, InfluxDB, Loki, PostgreSQL and many others. It's widely used by infrastructure and development teams, and can be self-hosted to maintain full control of information.

features.1: Interactive dashboards with real-time graphs and metrics
features.2: Customizable alerts and support for various data sources
features.3: Integration with Prometheus, Loki, InfluxDB, PostgreSQL and more

grafana.reasonToUse: Use Grafana if you already collect metrics with Prometheus or another system and need powerful visual dashboards — it's the standard visualization tool in the open source observability ecosystem.

challenges.1: Grafana by itself does not collect metrics — you need a separate data source like Prometheus or InfluxDB, which increases infrastructure complexity.
challenges.2: Advanced alerts, SAML/OAuth authentication and granular folder permissions are only available in Grafana Enterprise (paid).
challenges.3: Complex dashboards with many simultaneous queries can overload the metrics database — optimizing PromQL queries requires specialized knowledge.

prometheus.description: A monitoring and metrics collection system, used to track the health of servers and applications.
prometheus.longDescription: Prometheus is a monitoring tool that collects, stores and queries metrics from systems, applications, containers and services. It uses a time-series data model and its own language (PromQL) for creating alerts and analyses. It's widely used in Docker and Kubernetes environments, and can be integrated with Grafana for visualization. Being self-hosted, you have full control over the metrics and alerts in your environment.

features.1: Automatic metrics collection in time-series format
features.2: Alert system with custom rules
features.3: Integration with Grafana for dashboard visualization

prometheus.reasonToUse: Use Prometheus if you operate Docker or Kubernetes infrastructure and need time-series metric monitoring — it's the industry standard in that ecosystem.

challenges.1: Prometheus local storage is not suitable for long-term retention — environments that need months of history should use solutions like Thanos or VictoriaMetrics.
challenges.2: PromQL has a real learning curve — writing efficient queries for complex alerts requires practice and familiarity with the data model.
challenges.3: Prometheus has no native authentication — exposing the interface without a reverse proxy with authentication means exposing all metrics publicly.

funkwhale.description: A decentralized platform for listening to and sharing music, like a federated Spotify.
funkwhale.longDescription: Funkwhale is an audio streaming platform that lets you host, organize and share music in a decentralized way. It operates with federated instances using ActivityPub, allowing you to follow and listen to content from other instances, like on Mastodon. It can be used for personal collections, community radio stations or collaborative catalogs. It's a great Spotify alternative, but focused on freedom and data control.

features.1: Audio streaming with web interface and personal collection support
features.2: Integration with the Fediverse via ActivityPub
features.3: Ideal for community radio stations, independent artists or personal use

funkwhale.reasonToUse: Use Funkwhale if you're an independent artist or manage a music catalog and want to distribute your music without depending on platforms that take percentages of plays.

challenges.1: Installation requires Python, PostgreSQL, Redis and a file server — it's more complex than other Fediverse platforms.
challenges.2: The project has a slow development pace and the maintainer team is small — updates and bug fixes can take time.
challenges.3: Music discovery between instances is limited — federation with other Fediverse platforms is partial and can be inconsistent.

irc.description: A classic real-time chat protocol, used since the early days of the internet for group conversations.
irc.longDescription: IRC (Internet Relay Chat) is one of the oldest communication protocols on the internet, created for real-time chat. It allows people to join channels (chat rooms) or exchange direct messages on public or private servers. Despite its age, IRC is still used by many technical communities and open source software projects. It's lightweight, simple, works anywhere and can be hosted by you for full control.

features.1: Real-time chat rooms (channels) and direct messages
features.2: Lightweight, fast and works even on slow connections
features.3: Can be self-hosted and integrated with bots and scripts

irc.reasonToUse: Use IRC if you manage or participate in technical communities that already use this protocol — it's extremely lightweight, standardized and works in any client, from terminal to browser.

challenges.1: No message persistence by default — if you disconnect, you lose everything sent while offline (requires a bouncer or persistent client like The Lounge).
challenges.2: No native client-to-server encryption in many implementations — requires explicit TLS configuration and message content may be visible to the server administrator.
challenges.3: The experience is considered outdated by users accustomed to WhatsApp or Telegram — no link previews, reactions or synchronized history across devices.

thelounge.description: A modern web client for IRC, with a friendly interface and continuous access to conversations.
thelounge.longDescription: The Lounge is an IRC client that runs in the browser and keeps you always connected to channels, even when you close the tab. It's ideal for those who want to use IRC with a modern and practical interface, similar to Slack or Discord, while maintaining the lightness and decentralization of the IRC protocol. It's self-hostable and can be accessed from any device, with persistent history and support for multiple networks.

features.1: Beautiful and responsive web interface, inspired by modern apps
features.2: Continuous connection: you don't miss messages when offline
features.3: Support for multiple servers and complete conversation history

thelounge.reasonToUse: Use The Lounge if you use IRC regularly and want message persistence and a modern interface without giving up the classic protocol — it's the bouncer and client in one.

challenges.1: Requires an always-online server to function — unlike traditional IRC clients, it doesn't work as a standalone desktop app.
challenges.2: History storage grows continuously and can occupy considerable space in active conversations over time.
challenges.3: Setting up secure HTTPS access and authentication requires a reverse proxy (Nginx, Caddy) correctly configured in front of The Lounge.

lasuite.description: A suite of online office tools maintained by the French government, focused on digital sovereignty.
lasuite.longDescription: La Suite is an online application suite focused on communication and productivity, created and maintained by French public institutions. It includes email, calendar, document editor, video conferencing and other collaborative services. The project's goal is to offer a secure, sovereign and open source alternative to platforms like Google Workspace and Microsoft 365, promoting technological independence in public institutions.

features.1: Online document, spreadsheet and presentation editor
features.2: Integrated email, calendar, chat and video conferencing
features.3: Based on free software, focused on privacy and institutional control

lasuite.reasonToUse: Use La Suite if you are a public institution or company that wants to replace Google Workspace with a sovereign solution maintained by a democratic and auditable government.

challenges.1: The project is primarily aimed at French institutions — documentation, support and roadmap focus on the European context, with fewer resources for users from other countries.
challenges.2: Full self-hosting of all components requires integrating multiple services (Nextcloud, Jitsi, Mattermost, etc.) with high setup and maintenance costs.
challenges.3: Component maturity varies — some integrated services are more stable than others, and the unified experience is not yet as smooth as Google Workspace.
